title: 4x4 Truss Beam Bench
summary: A heavy 4x4-legged bench with splayed 10° legs and a wide 2x8 plank top — built to match a truss-style farmhouse table.
description:
This bench is meant to sit alongside a chunky farmhouse table, so it borrows the same visual language: solid 4x4 legs splayed outward, joined into truss-shaped end assemblies, carrying a wide top built from two 2x8 planks. The end assemblies are the one part that takes real care — each is built from a leg top, two angled legs, and an angled stretcher, all cut at 10° so they lean outward slightly rather than standing dead vertical, which is what gives the whole piece its truss-beam look instead of reading as a plain bench on four straight legs.

Build your bench top as its own flat assembly before attaching it to the base — glue and pocket-screw the two 2x8 planks together, add the end pieces (sized to exactly match your dining table if you're building this as a companion piece), then set the finished top onto the base and screw down through the aprons.

tools.note: Cuts the 10° angled legs and stretchers.

materials.name: 2x10 board, 3 ft
materials.note: Breadboard ends — 2x8 scraps work too if you have them.

cutList.note: Long point to short point, both ends cut at 10° off square, ends parallel.
Long point to long point, both ends cut at 10° off square, NOT parallel.
Size to exactly match your table if building as a companion piece.

steps.title: Build the two leg-end assemblies
steps.body: Join a leg top to two angled legs using pocket holes — there are a lot of ways to join 4x4s; a Kreg jig set for thick stock is the cleanest, since the pocket holes end up hidden underneath the assembly.

steps.title: Attach the bottom stretcher
steps.body: Attach the base stretcher between the two leg-end assemblies, checking the whole base sits flat and even before the joints are fully tightened.

steps.title: Add the side aprons
steps.body: Add the two side aprons — these give the seat structure and read as part of the 4x4 leg language from the outside even though they're 2x4. Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes facing upward on the inside of the aprons now, before attaching, so you can drive up into the bench top in the next step.

steps.title: Build and attach the top
steps.body: Build the bench top first, as its own assembly: pocket-screw the two 2x8 planks together edge to edge, then attach the breadboard ends. Once it's built, set it onto the base and screw up through the apron pocket holes to attach it.

images.alt: Heavy 4x4-legged truss bench with a wide plank top
